name: infertility; apaosha (demon of drought)
shortDescription: Apoosha is the Avestan demon of drought, locked in eternal conflict with Tishtrya, the star divinity associated with rainfall and agriculture.
description: Apoosha, in Zoroastrian mythology, is a powerful demon embodying drought and aridity. He is the antithesis of fertility and abundance, representing the destructive force that withholds vital water from the land. His primary opponent is Tishtrya, a Yazata (angelic being) who embodies the life-giving rains. The battles between Apoosha and Tishtrya are central to understanding the Zoroastrian worldview, which emphasizes the constant struggle between good and evil, order and chaos. Apoosha's power manifests as prolonged droughts, causing famine, infertility in both land and people, and widespread suffering. The defeat of Apoosha by Tishtrya brings about the much-needed rains, restoring life and prosperity to the world. This conflict highlights the importance of water in the ancient Persian worldview and the constant need to combat the forces of destruction to ensure survival. Apoosha's existence emphasizes the precariousness of life and the ever-present threat of environmental disaster.
